text: and anthropology alike: liminality has come to stand for a cultural hybridity that entertains difference without an assumed or imposed hierarchy. In anthropology such a position of hybridity was embraced in an attitude of distance-taking towards ‘classical anthropology’ which had – allegedly – fixed persons and cultures in essentialized time-space units, reifying the world before us. Liminality, with one stroke of magic, undoes all that, dissolving any fixity of position, dissolving the modern into permanent hybridity. In recent years, this notion of liminality as creative hybridity has entered the wider vocabularies of the political sciences. As we have discussed elsewhere in the context of the multiple modernities ‘paradigm’ (Thomassen 2012c), to pluralize and ‘write into history’ hitherto excluded subjects by discovering ‘hybrid’ or ‘alternative modernities’ has by both writers and social theorists been employed as a means of resistance to the new global (neo-liberal) order, and a call for new forms of justice and inclusion. Into Liminality 9 But at the heart of our celebratory attitudes toward pluralities of the modern lies a theoretical misconception that needs to be spelled out with some clarity. This misconception rests on the conclusion reached in the 1980s, namely that ‘old’ modernity represented a uniformizing/rationalizing force. It is against this background that plural modernities, liminality and hybridity of forms become both ‘good’ and necessary. It is certainly true that in the social sciences the dominance of modernization theories in the post-war period led to a view of modernity as uniform across time and space, equating it with Western ideals, ideas and institutions. The problem is that this idea of modernity as unitary and uniformizing was always wrong. It is even conceptually wrong. The very notion of being modern was, from its inception in the early sixteenth century precisely not about creating ‘sameness’, as it indeed demarcated a difference from the past (the ancients), and a notion of continuously evolving horizons. The modern was the new, the diverse, the forward-looking: it had no definite form and no definite end result, other than a continuously moving horizon; it was about change and movement, speeding up, dissolving and transforming everything at hand (Koselleck 1979; Wagner 2008; Giesen 2009). That is also why we hesitate to adopt Bauman’s view that modernity was once ‘hard’, before it eventually became liquid (in ‘postmodernity’). It is rather the case that the ‘hardness’ of modernity was from its inception built upon and tied to its seeming opposite, as a co-constituting element rather than an alternative: the out- of-the-ordinary, the transgressive, the liminal. Victor Turner himself played a huge role in this positioning of hybridity and liminality as emancipatory tool. One of his most read essays ever, The Anthropology of Performance (1988), opens with a dream-like wish: ‘For years I have dreamed of a liberated anthropology’. Turner came to see ‘post-modernism’ as the key to that liberation, and liminality the one central concept to unfold within that vision. One part of Turner’s assessment of the social sciences is difficult to disagree with. Anthropology embedded in the modern worldview, from evolutionism to functionalism and structuralism, involved ‘a systematic dehumanizing of the human subjects of study, regarding them as bearers of an impersonal “culture” [...], determined by social, cultural or social psychological “forces”, “variables”, or “pressures”’ (ibid.: 72). This modern, scientific worldview now belongs to the past, says Turner (ibid.: 72). Instead we have a more open discourse with a new blending of genres and disciplinary boundary crossings. The obsessions with ‘fit and congruence’ (ibid.: 74) is replaced with Turner’s own view of the field as a ‘set of loosely integrated processes, with some patterned aspects, some persistences of form, but controlled by discrepant principles of action expressed in rules of custom that are often situationally incompatible with one another’ (ibid.). Turner’s position here is a neat expression of the view, established since the 1980s, that postmodernism involved a liberation of genres and a going beyond the rationalizing and confining tendency of the modern world-view. This postmodern turn, says Turner, ‘involves the processualization of space, its temporalization, as against the spatialization of process or time, which we found to be the essence Liminality and the Modern 10 of the modern’ (ibid.: 76). As we shall discuss further, Turner placed this view in the method called ‘social drama analysis’, linking it to a broader paradigmatic shift towards a processual social science which sees social interaction itself as theatrical and staged in Goffman’s sense; a kind of social science that opens up the field toward the performative genres, including literature, theatre and arts. Social dramas are here defined by Turner as ‘units of aharmonic or disharmonic social process, arising in conflict situations’ (ibid.). And it is this process that Turner engaged, equipped with the tripartite structure of van Gennep’s ritual passage. In so far as this paves the ground for Turner’s incredibly useful analysis of social dramas in their unfolding, this is all well-taken and hugely important. However, Turner’s own analysis of the kind of ‘liberty’ that explodes in liminality should perhaps have made him more reflexive with regard to the generative powers of liminality. When all is said and done, Turner preferred to see liminality as a fascinating and necessary shaking of routines, a cultural space of human creativity. As we shall discuss more in detail, it was in this vein that Turner coined the term ‘liminoid’ to denote how liminal practices re-emerged in modern consumer societies via art, theatre and the larger leisure culture industry. This again involves a celebratory stance towards the non-systemic and towards creativity, play and fantasy. However, here Turner simplifies matters, and we shall argue that his cultural analysis needs a different kind of sociological rooting. Moreover, much like Homi Bhabha, he translates an analytical position into a normative ideal. Turner’s take on modernity is also misleading in a very fundamental way, and this is because the modern was never just about rationalizing, measuring and categorizing: it was in a quite fundamental sense equally about ‘play and fantasy’, and it was,
